file_name: Islam et al. - 2024 - BT13 (P028) The importance of clinical metadata when triaging patients for face-to-face assessment a.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly states it integrates five machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest, and multilayer perceptron) to classify suspicious skin lesions based on patient metadata in a teledermatology pathway.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is labeled as a conference abstract (BT13 (P028)) presenting original experimental results. It describes a study analyzing clinical metadata from 53,601 skin lesions in 25,105 patients to evaluate machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, MLP) for suspicious skin lesion detection, reporting specific performance metrics like sensitivity (85.2%).

title: The importance of clinical metadata when triaging patients for face-to-face assessment and possible biopsy in a teledermatology pathway
brief_summary: This study investigates the potential of machine learning models utilizing clinical metadata for detecting suspicious skin lesions in a teledermatology pathway. The researchers analyzed metadata from 53,601 skin lesions in 25,105 patients from private UK clinics between 2015 and 2022. Five ML models, including naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, and MLP, were integrated to classify lesions as suspicious or nonsuspicious based on patient metadata. The evaluation compared model performance using the original 7-point checklist (7PCL) versus adding various meta-features, utilizing metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and balanced accuracy. The results showed that while there were no significant differences between original and weighted 7PCL, adding 11 specific meta-features significantly improved performance, achieving a sensitivity of 85.2%. The study concludes that fusing high-performing meta-features with image modalities could further boost detection and modify referral guidelines.

lesion_types_studied.details: Lesions categorized as suspicious (10% of dataset) by skin cancer specialists during telemedicine triage, with a cancer detection rate of 5%. The study focuses on detecting these lesions for possible biopsy.
Lesions categorized as nonsuspicious (90% of dataset) by skin cancer specialists during telemedicine triage.

Mentioned in the context of the NICE 7-point checklist (7PCL) recommendation for selecting patients with pigmented lesions and possible melanoma for urgent referral, but the study dataset included all skin lesions (pigmented and nonpigmented).

Mentioned as a condition for urgent referral via 7PCL and as a meta-feature (prior melanoma history) in the Williams score characteristics. The study does not report specific counts or performance metrics for melanoma vs. other cancers, but rather for the binary 'suspicious' vs 'nonsuspicious' classification.
Included as a meta-feature (history of prior nonmelanoma skin cancer) in the Williams score characteristics.
Included as a meta-feature (whether the lesion was predominantly nonpigmented pink) and as one of the 11 meta-features added to improve model performance.

all_task_types.details: Binary classification task where the model predicts whether a skin lesion is 'suspicious' (10% of dataset) or 'nonsuspicious' (90% of dataset) based on clinical metadata. The study integrates five ML models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, MLP) to classify these discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: Private UK Teledermatology Clinic Dataset
datasets_used.description: Clinical metadata from 53,601 skin lesions in 25,105 patients who attended private skin cancer diagnosis clinics in the UK between 2015 and 2022. The data was collected via a teledermatology pathway involving professional photography and specialist triage.

use.training: Used as input for five machine learning models (naive Bayes, logistic regression, SVM, random forest, MLP) to classify lesions based on clinical metadata.
use.testing: Used to evaluate model performance using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, and AUC.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of model performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of machine learning models using original 7-point checklist (7PCL) versus weighted 7PCL, and to evaluate the performance gain when adding individual or combined meta-features.

statistical_methods_used.method: Statistical significance testing (implied by 'no significant differences' and 'improved significantly')
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine if the differences in model performance between the original 7PCL and weighted 7PCL were statistically significant, and to confirm the significant improvement in performance when adding 11 specific meta-features.

hypothesis_testing.test: Statistical significance testing (specific name not reported, but described as comparing performance metrics)
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in model performance (sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, AUC) between the original 7-point checklist (7PCL) and the weighted 7PCL.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in model performance between the original 7PCL and the weighted 7PCL, or adding specific meta-features significantly improves performance compared to 7PCL alone.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No significant differences were observed in model performance between the original 7PCL and weighted 7PCL (sensitivity 68.1%). However, the performance of the ML model was improved significantly for each added meta-feature, with the best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) observed when 11 specific meta-features were added to the 7PCL.

Private UK Teledermatology Clinic Datase.notes: There were no significant differences observed in model performance between the original 7PCL and weighted 7PCL. The performance of the ML model was improved significantly for each added meta-feature. The best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) was observed when 11 specific meta-features (lesion pink, Williams score, lesion age, sunburn, patient age, Williams group, patient sex, hair colour, site of the lesion on the body, freckling tendency, and mole count) were added to the 7PCL. Five ML models were integrated: naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine, random forest, and multilayer perceptron. Evaluation metrics included balanced accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and area under the curve.

architecture_used.main_model: Five machine learning models: naive Bayes, logistic regression, support vector machine (SVM), random forest, and multilayer perceptron (MLP).

input_features: Williams score characteristics (patient age, patient sex, natural hair colour, arm mole count, sunburn history, prior nonmelanoma skin cancer)
best_feature_set: site of the lesion on the body

conclusions: There were no significant differences in model performance between the original 7-point checklist (7PCL) and the weighted 7PCL for detecting suspicious skin lesions (sensitivity 68.1%).
The performance of machine learning models for categorizing suspicious skin lesions was significantly improved by adding specific meta-features to the 7PCL.

The best performance gain (sensitivity 85.2%) was observed when 11 specific meta-features were added to the 7PCL: lesion pink, Williams score, lesion age, sunburn, patient age, Williams group, patient sex, hair colour, site of the lesion on the body, freckling tendency, and mole count.
Fusing high-performing clinical meta-features with image modalities is likely to further boost machine learning model performance for skin cancer detection.
The identified high-performing meta-features could be used to modify current skin cancer referral guidelines.
Ongoing engagement with AI solutions is likely required to develop skin cancer services fit for future demand and address clinician concerns.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (BT13 (P028)) published in the British Journal of Dermatology, which may limit the depth of methodological detail and statistical reporting compared to a full peer-reviewed article.

The dataset is derived exclusively from private UK skin cancer diagnosis clinics between 2015 and 2022, introducing potential selection bias as it may not represent the general population or NHS teledermatology pathways.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with suspicious lesions comprising only 10% of the 53,601 lesions analyzed, which can skew performance metrics if not handled appropriately.

The cancer detection rate in the dataset was 5%, suggesting that the 'suspicious' label by specialists may not perfectly correlate with confirmed malignancy, potentially affecting the ground truth validity.
The paper does not report specific confidence intervals or p-values for the performance improvements, relying only on qualitative statements of 'significant' improvement.

The study focuses on clinical metadata and does not evaluate the integration of image modalities, despite concluding that fusing metadata with images would likely boost performance; thus, the actual clinical utility in a multimodal setting is unverified.
No external validation on independent datasets or different healthcare systems was performed, limiting the generalizability of the identified 11 meta-features.

The study acknowledges ongoing clinician concerns and administrative burdens (e.g., waiting lists, GP referral discrepancies) which are critical contextual factors for deployment but are not addressed by the ML model itself.
